concepts.name: gower dissimilarity
concepts.description: A general dissimilarity coefficient for mixed data types (quantitative, ordinal, and categorical) that combines different attribute-specific dissimilarities.
concepts.existing_quote: A general dissimilarity coefficient due to Gower (1971) handles all attribute types. For each attribute, a dissimilarity dj(xij, xi′j) is computed, and these are combined into an overall dissimilarity.
concepts.existing_formula: d(x_i, x_{i'}) = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{p} w_{ij} d_j(x_{ij}, x_{i'j})}{\sum_{j=1}^{p} w_{ij}}

14
Unsupervised Learning

The previous chapters have been concerned with predicting the values
of one or more outputs or response variables Y
= (Y1, . . . , Ym) for a
given set of input or predictor variables XT = (X1, . . . , Xp). Denote by
xT
i
= (xi1, . . . , xip) the inputs for the ith training case, and let yi be a
response measurement. The predictions are based on the training sample
(x1, y1), . . . , (xN, yN) of previously solved cases, where the joint values of
all of the variables are known. This is called supervised learning or “learn-
ing with a teacher.” Under this metaphor the “student” presents an an-
swer ˆyi for each xi in the training sample, and the supervisor or “teacher”
provides either the correct answer and/or an error associated with the stu-
dent’s answer. This is usually characterized by some loss function L(y, ˆy),
for example, L(y, ˆy) = (y −ˆy)2.
If one supposes that (X, Y ) are random variables represented by some
joint probability density Pr(X, Y ), then supervised learning can be formally
characterized as a density estimation problem where one is concerned with
determining properties of the conditional density Pr(Y |X). Usually the
properties of interest are the “location” parameters μ that minimize the
expected error at each x,

where Pr(X) is the joint marginal density of the X values alone. In su-
pervised learning Pr(X) is typically of no direct concern. One is interested
mainly in the properties of the conditional density Pr(Y |X). Since Y is of-
ten of low dimension (usually one), and only its location μ(x) is of interest,
the problem is greatly simpliﬁed. As discussed in the previous chapters,
there are many approaches for successfully addressing supervised learning
in a variety of contexts.
In this chapter we address unsupervised learning or “learning without a
teacher.” In this case one has a set of N observations (x1, x2, . . . , xN) of a
random p-vector X having joint density Pr(X). The goal is to directly infer
the properties of this probability density without the help of a supervisor or
teacher providing correct answers or degree-of-error for each observation.
The dimension of X is sometimes much higher than in supervised learn-
ing, and the properties of interest are often more complicated than simple
location estimates. These factors are somewhat mitigated by the fact that
X represents all of the variables under consideration; one is not required
to infer how the properties of Pr(X) change, conditioned on the changing
values of another set of variables.
In low-dimensional problems (say p ≤3), there are a variety of eﬀective
nonparametric methods for directly estimating the density Pr(X) itself at
all X-values, and representing it graphically (Silverman, 1986, e.g.). Owing
to the curse of dimensionality, these methods fail in high dimensions. One
must settle for estimating rather crude global models, such as Gaussian
mixtures or various simple descriptive statistics that characterize Pr(X).
Generally, these descriptive statistics attempt to characterize X-values,
or collections of such values, where Pr(X) is relatively large. Principal
components, multidimensional scaling, self-organizing maps, and principal
curves, for example, attempt to identify low-dimensional manifolds within
the X-space that represent high data density. This provides information
about the associations among the variables and whether or not they can be
considered as functions of a smaller set of “latent” variables. Cluster anal-
ysis attempts to ﬁnd multiple convex regions of the X-space that contain
modes of Pr(X). This can tell whether or not Pr(X) can be represented by
a mixture of simpler densities representing distinct types or classes of ob-
servations. Mixture modeling has a similar goal. Association rules attempt
to construct simple descriptions (conjunctive rules) that describe regions
of high density in the special case of very high dimensional binary-valued
data.
With supervised learning there is a clear measure of success, or lack
thereof, that can be used to judge adequacy in particular situations and
to compare the eﬀectiveness of diﬀerent methods over various situations.

concepts.name: object dissimilarity
concepts.description: Dissimilarity measures that treat observations as unstructured objects without explicit attributes, measured directly between observations.
concepts.existing_quote: In some applications the features are not used at all. Rather, the user supplies an N × N matrix of pairwise dissimilarities among the observations, based on domain knowledge.

concepts.name: combinatorial clustering algorithms
concepts.description: Clustering algorithms that work directly with observed data or dissimilarities and assign observations to clusters.
concepts.existing_quote: Combinatorial algorithms work directly with the observed data or pairwise dissimilarities. They produce a partition of the data, assigning each observation to one and only one cluster.

concepts.name: mixture modeling clustering
concepts.description: A probabilistic clustering approach that assumes data is generated from a mixture of underlying probability distributions.
concepts.existing_quote: Mixture modeling assumes that the data are generated by a mixture of underlying probability distributions. Each component distribution represents a cluster.

concepts.name: mode seeking clustering
concepts.description: Clustering methods that attempt to directly estimate distinct modes of the probability density function, with each mode representing a cluster.
concepts.existing_quote: Mode seeking ('bump hunting') methods take a nonparametric perspective, attempting to directly estimate distinct modes of the probability density function. Observations 'closest' to each respective mode then define the individual clusters.

concepts.name: hierarchical clustering
concepts.description: Clustering methods that produce a sequence of nested partitions from fine (all singletons) to coarse (all observations in one cluster), often represented as a dendrogram.
concepts.existing_quote: Hierarchical clustering produces a nested sequence of partitions, from single observation clusters at the bottom to one cluster containing all observations at the top. A dendrogram represents this hierarchy.

concepts.name: agglomerative clustering
concepts.description: A bottom-up hierarchical clustering approach that starts with each observation as its own cluster and successively merges closest pairs.
concepts.existing_quote: Agglomerative (bottom-up) clustering starts with each observation in its own cluster. The two closest clusters are merged, and this is repeated until there is only one cluster.

concepts.name: divisive clustering
concepts.description: Top-down hierarchical clustering starting with all data in one cluster, recursively splitting clusters to maximize between-group dissimilarity.
concepts.existing_quote: Divisive (top-down) clustering starts with all observations in one cluster. The cluster is split into two, and this is repeated recursively until each observation is in its own cluster.

concepts.name: single linkage
concepts.description: A dissimilarity measure between two groups defined as the minimum dissimilarity between any observation in one group and any observation in the other.
concepts.existing_quote: Single linkage (nearest-neighbor) defines the dissimilarity between two groups as the minimum dissimilarity between any observation in one group and any observation in the other: d_SL(G, H) = min_{i∈G, i′∈H} d_{ii′}.
concepts.existing_formula: d_{SL}(G, H) = \min_{i \in G, i' \in H} d_{ii'}

concepts.name: complete linkage
concepts.description: A dissimilarity measure between two groups defined as the maximum dissimilarity between any observation in one group and any observation in the other.
concepts.existing_quote: Complete linkage (furthest-neighbor) defines the dissimilarity as the maximum dissimilarity: d_CL(G, H) = max_{i∈G, i′∈H} d_{ii′}.

concepts.name: average linkage
concepts.description: A dissimilarity measure between two groups defined as the average dissimilarity between all pairs of observations from the two groups.
concepts.existing_quote: Group average defines the dissimilarity as the average of all pairwise dissimilarities: d_GA(G, H) = (1/(N_G × N_H)) ∑_{i∈G} ∑_{i′∈H} d_{ii′}.
concepts.existing_formula: d_{GA}(G, H) = \frac{1}{N_G N_H} \sum_{i \in G} \sum_{i' \in H} d_{ii'}

concepts.name: Ward's method
concepts.description: An agglomerative hierarchical clustering method that minimizes the within-cluster sum of squares at each merge, similar to K-means objective.
concepts.existing_quote: Ward's method uses an ANOVA-based approach that minimizes the within-cluster sum of squares. At each step, merge the pair of clusters that leads to the minimum increase in total within-cluster variance.

concepts.name: codeword
concepts.description: The cluster centroid used to approximate a block of pixels in vector quantization; the collection of codewords forms the codebook.
concepts.existing_quote: Each of the 512 × 512 pixel blocks (or points) is approximated by its closest cluster centroid, known as a codeword.

concepts.description: In vector quantization, the clustering process that assigns each data block to its nearest codeword.
concepts.existing_quote: The clustering process is called the encoding step, and the collection of centroids is called the codebook.

concepts.name: rate-distortion tradeoff
concepts.description: In lossy compression, the tradeoff between compression rate (bits per pixel) and distortion (reconstruction error).

of one or more outputs or response variables Y

xT
i
= (xi1, . . . , xip) the inputs for the ith training case, and let yi be a
response measurement. The predictions are based on the training sample

concepts.name: variable length coding
concepts.description: A coding scheme that assigns shorter codes to more frequent codewords, using entropy-based Shannon coding theory.
concepts.existing_quote: Using Shannon coding theory, we know that in general a variable length code will do better, and the rate then becomes −∑K ℓ=1 pℓlog2(pℓ)/4. The term in the numerator is the entropy of the distribution pℓ of the codewords in the image.

concepts.description: The Shannon entropy of the distribution of codewords in variable length coding, determining the optimal compression rate.

concepts.name: tree-structured vector quantization
concepts.description: A generalization of VQ that finds centroids with a top-down, 2-means style algorithm, allowing successive refinement of compression.
concepts.existing_quote: For example, tree-structured VQ finds the centroids with a top-down, 2-means style algorithm, as alluded to in Section 14.3.12. This allows successive refinement of the compression.

concepts.name: k-medoids clustering
concepts.description: Clustering method restricting cluster centers to actual observations, minimizing total dissimilarity to cluster members using arbitrary dissimilarity measures.
concepts.existing_quote: K-medoids clustering is a variant of K-means where the cluster representatives are restricted to be observations in the dataset. For each cluster, find the observation that minimizes the total distance to other points in that cluster.

concepts.name: silhouette coefficient
concepts.description: A measure of clustering quality that compares within-cluster tightness to between-cluster separation for each observation.
concepts.existing_quote: The silhouette coefficient s(i) for observation i is defined as s(i) = (b(i) - a(i))/max{a(i), b(i)}, where a(i) is the average dissimilarity of i to all other objects in the same cluster, and b(i) is the minimum average dissimilarity of i to objects in other clusters.
